review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.summary: executeSurfacePattern bypasses QueryExecutor interface via repeated downcasts in api and query layers

evidence: WordSketchApiServer.java lines 306, 350, 403, 455, 693-694: ((BlackLabQueryExecutor) executor).executeSurfacePattern(...) — all five main sketch handler paths cast through the interface

SemanticFieldExplorer.java line 121-123: if (executor instanceof BlackLabQueryExecutor) { ((BlackLabQueryExecutor) executor).executeSurfacePattern(...) } — the query layer also punches through its own abstraction
QueryExecutor.java: executeSurfacePattern is absent from the interface, making it structurally unreachable without a downcast
detail.suggestion: Add executeSurfacePattern(String lemma, String bcqlPattern, int headPosition, int collocatePosition, double minLogDice, int maxResults) as a default method on QueryExecutor (throwing UnsupportedOperationException or delegating to findCollocations as the fallback), then remove all (BlackLabQueryExecutor) casts in WordSketchApiServer and SemanticFieldExplorer. This eliminates six boundary violations and makes the abstraction honest.

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.summary: tagging package imports indexer.LuceneIndexer, reversing the natural pipeline dependency direction

CorpusProcessor.java line 5: import pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.indexer.LuceneIndexer; line 23: private final LuceneIndexer indexer;

review::.::holistic::package_organizatio.summary: indexer/hybrid/ (22 files) mixes 5 distinct concern groups with no sub-package separation

evidence: 22 Java files in a single flat directory, exceeding the >10 mixed-concern threshold.

Five distinct concern groups identified: (1) processing pipeline — HybridIndexer, HybridConllUProcessor, SinglePassConlluProcessor, CollocationsBuilder; (2) domain model records — Collocation, CollocationEntry, TermStatistics, SentenceDocument; (3) codec/binary-IO — LemmaIdsCodec, LemmaLexiconReader, LemmaLexiconWriter, PairRunIO, TokenSequenceCodec, IntArrayBuffer; (4) statistics — StatisticsIndexBuilder, StatisticsReader, StatisticsConverter; (5) utilities/migration — LexiconFromStatsMigrator, LongIntHashMap, LongIntPairSort, LemmaIdAssigner, PositionalTokenStream.
CollocationsBuilder alone is 1139 LOC sitting adjacent to tiny data-carrier records (Collocation at ~40 LOC), making importance and ownership opaque.
detail.suggestion: Stage a reorg in three passes: (1) Create indexer/hybrid/model/ and move Collocation.java, CollocationEntry.java, TermStatistics.java, SentenceDocument.java — update package declarations and all imports (only internal to indexer/hybrid and CollocationsBuilder need updating). Validate: mvn compile. (2) Create indexer/hybrid/codec/ and move LemmaIdsCodec.java, LemmaLexiconReader.java, LemmaLexiconWriter.java, PairRunIO.java, TokenSequenceCodec.java, IntArrayBuffer.java. Validate: mvn compile. (3) Create indexer/hybrid/stats/ and move StatisticsIndexBuilder.java, StatisticsReader.java, StatisticsConverter.java; move LongIntHashMap.java, LongIntPairSort.java, LexiconFromStatsMigrator.java, LemmaIdAssigner.java, PositionalTokenStream.java to indexer/hybrid/util/. Validate: mvn test. The top-level processing classes (HybridIndexer, CollocationsBuilder, HybridConllUProcessor, SinglePassConlluProcessor) remain directly under indexer/hybrid/ as they are the primary entry points.

review::.::holistic::initialization_coup.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer opened per-request in API server, bypassing the shared executor and leaking on exceptions

evidence: WordSketchApiServer.java line ~530: 'SemanticFieldExplorer explorer = new SemanticFieldExplorer(indexPath);' — this calls SemanticFieldExplorer(String), which calls 'new BlackLabQueryExecutor(indexPath)' which calls 'BlackLab.open(new File(indexPath))', opening the index from disk on every request.
The same pattern appears at lines ~786 and ~864 for compare and explore-multi paths.
In all three sites close() is called only on the happy path — if 'explorer.exploreByPattern(...)' throws, the index handle is never closed.
WordSketchApiServer already holds 'this.executor' (a shared BlackLabQueryExecutor) which could be passed to the testable 'SemanticFieldExplorer(QueryExecutor)' constructor instead.
detail.suggestion: Pass this.executor to SemanticFieldExplorer at all three call sites: 'try (SemanticFieldExplorer explorer = new SemanticFieldExplorer(this.executor))'. This reuses the already-open shared index, removes the per-request I/O, and the try-with-resources guarantees close() even on exception paths.

review::.::holistic::type_safety::map_ob.summary: All API response objects built as Map<String, Object> despite typed domain classes existing

SemanticFieldExplorer.ExplorationResult, DiscoveredNoun, and CoreAdjective are fully-typed classes yet the API manually copies each field into a Map, e.g. lines 563-614 for the explore response.
There is no compile-time guarantee that a field added to DiscoveredNoun will appear in the API response — the mapping code can silently omit or misspell field names.
detail.suggestion: Annotate the domain result classes (ExplorationResult, DiscoveredNoun, WordSketchResult, etc.) with fastjson2 `@JSONField` or ensure they are serializable by fastjson2's reflection, then pass the typed objects directly to `sendJsonResponse()`. Remove the manual Map-building code. Alternatively, introduce dedicated response record types (e.g., ExploreResponse) that fastjson2 can serialize directly.

review::.::holistic::authorization_consi.summary: sendError() omits Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * header, breaking cross-origin error visibility on every endpoint

evidence: sendJsonResponse() sets exchange.getResponseHeaders().set("Access-Control-Allow-Origin", "*") before writing the body, establishing an open CORS policy for all success responses.
sendError() (lines ~1215-1228) sets Content-Type and the status code but never sets Access-Control-Allow-Origin. All ten registered endpoint contexts call sendError() for 400/405/500 paths.

The result: a browser making a cross-origin request receives a CORS error on any failure path, hiding the actual HTTP error code and JSON error body from the client, while the same request succeeds transparently on the happy path.
detail.suggestion: Add `exchange.getResponseHeaders().set("Access-Control-Allow-Origin", "*");` as the first header line inside sendError(), immediately after the method signature — one-line fix that aligns the error path's trust boundary with the success path's.

evidence: Lines 306, 350, 403, 455, 694 of WordSketchApiServer.java all contain ((BlackLabQueryExecutor) executor).executeSurfacePattern(...) — five forced downcasts to reach functionality not declared in the interface.
executeSurfacePattern(String lemma, String bcqlPattern, int headPosition, int collocatePosition, double minLogDice, int maxResults) is defined only on BlackLabQueryExecutor, not on QueryExecutor.

The five handler methods (handleFullSketch, handleRelationQuery, handleFullDependencySketch, handleDependencyRelationQuery, handleSemanticField) all depend on the cast, meaning any alternative QueryExecutor implementation would break these handlers at runtime.
detail.suggestion: Promote executeSurfacePattern() into the QueryExecutor interface (possibly with a default implementation that throws UnsupportedOperationException or delegates to findCollocations). This removes the need for all 5 casts and restores honest interface coverage. Alternatively, introduce a SurfacePatternQueryExecutor sub-interface that extends QueryExecutor and add an instanceof guard, but the cleaner solution is interface promotion.

review::.::holistic::mid_level_elegance:.summary: BlackLabQueryExecutor has five overlapping regex-based XML extraction helpers at the BlackLab→domain boundary

review::.::holistic::mid_level_elegance:.summary: POS-to-group mapping is implemented three times across three files with no shared root

evidence: PosTagger.TaggedToken.getPosGroup() maps Penn Treebank first char (N→noun, V→verb, J→adj, R→adv, D→det, P→pron, I→prep, C→conj, U→punct)
ConllUProcessor.mapUPOStoGroup() maps Universal POS strings (NOUN/PROPN/PRON→noun, VERB→verb, ADJ→adj, AUX→verb, CCONJ/SCONJ→conj) with different groupings
ConllUProcessor.guessUPOS() converts Penn Treebank tags back to Universal POS using yet another first-char switch, creating a round-trip path that is inconsistent with TaggedToken.getPosGroup()
detail.suggestion: Introduce a single PosGroupMapper utility class (or static methods on a shared PosUtils class) with two clearly named factory methods: fromPennTreebank(String xpos) and fromUniversalPos(String upos). Both ConllUProcessor and TaggedToken should delegate to this shared class. This puts tag translation at one obvious place and eliminates the three diverging implementations.

review::.::holistic::contract_coherence:.summary: word1.replace("&","&") at line 851 is called before the null-check at line 856, causing NullPointerException when word1 is absent from query params

evidence: Line 847: 'String word1 = params.get("word1");' — returns null if key absent
Line 851: 'if (word1 != null) word1 = word1.replace("&", "&");' — actually this line IS guarded... wait re-reading: 'if (word1 != null) word1 = word1.replace(...)' — actually checking again
Line 851: 'if (word1 != null) word1 = word1.replace("&", "&");' — the null guard IS present here
However word2 at line 852 also has the same pattern: 'if (word2 != null) word2 = word2.replace("&", "&");' — also guarded

The null checks at lines 851-852 guard the replace calls correctly; the contract issue is the HTML-entity unescaping logic placed in a URL-query-param handler: '&' is an HTML entity, not a URL-encoded character, and should not appear in URL query strings
detail.suggestion: Remove the & replacement. URL-encoded ampersands are decoded by URLDecoder as '%26' → '&', not as '&'. If clients send malformed HTML-encoded URLs, validate and reject at the client layer.

review::.::holistic::authorization_consi.summary: No authentication or authorization is applied to any endpoint; wildcard CORS is universal

evidence: All 10+ endpoint handlers proceed directly to business logic with no token validation, API key check, or session verification
Every response sets Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * (HttpApiUtils lines 28, 44, 56, 67), permitting cross-origin requests from any domain
No request IP restriction, rate limiting, or audit logging exists anywhere in the server
detail.suggestion: If this server is intended for local use only, add a check that the remote address is loopback (127.0.0.1 / ::1) and reject other connections with 403. If broader deployment is planned, add token-based authentication before the application reaches production use. The pattern is at least consistent (no partial enforcement), but the wildcard CORS combined with no auth allows any page on the internet to make requests if the port is reachable.

review::.::holistic::dependency_health::.summary: com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpServer is a JDK internal API used as the production HTTP server, risking portability and longevity

evidence: WordSketchApiServer declares 'private com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpServer server;' (line 56) and creates it via 'com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpServer.create(new InetSocketAddress(port), 0)' (line 74).
Handler registration uses 'com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpServer httpServer' as parameter type (line 185) and 'com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpHandler' as the handler type (line 186).

com.sun.* packages are internal JDK packages, not part of the public Java SE API. While widely available in practice, they are not guaranteed across all JDK vendors (e.g., GraalVM native image, IBM J9) and have been subject to removal or encapsulation in past JDK releases.
detail.suggestion: Replace com.sun.net.httpserver with a proper lightweight HTTP server dependency such as Javalin (which embeds Jetty), Eclipse Jetty embedded, or — if zero added dependencies is preferred — use the jdk.httpserver module explicitly declared in module-info.java (Java 9+ makes the server available via jdk.httpserver module, which is at least explicitly declared rather than a com.sun internal). Add the chosen server as a dependency in pom.xml so the dependency is explicit and version-pinned.

review::.::holistic::low_level_elegance:.summary: CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens uses a naive indexOf(']') that fails for ']' inside quoted strings within brackets

evidence: Line 44: 'int end = pattern.indexOf(']', i);' — this finds the first ']' after '[', even if that ']' is inside a quoted attribute value like [lemma="a]b"].
The comment 'Skip quoted string to avoid mistaking embedded content for tokens' only handles standalone quoted strings at the outer level, not quotes nested inside brackets.
A pattern like 1:[xpos="[NN.*]"] would be split incorrectly: the method would return [xpos="[NN.*] as one token, then treat ]"] as leftover text.
detail.suggestion: Rewrite splitCqlTokens to track both bracket nesting depth and whether the scanner is currently inside a double-quoted string (toggling on unescaped '"' characters). The '[' only opens a token when depth == 0, and ']' only closes the current token when depth == 1 and not inside a quote.

review::.::holistic::logic_clarity::find.summary: findCollocations silently discards the provided cqlPattern when it neither contains '%s' nor starts with '['

evidence: The three-branch condition: if (cqlPattern.contains('%s')) { ... } else if (cqlPattern.startsWith('[')) { ... } else { bcql = String.format('"headword" []', ...); } — the else branch ignores cqlPattern entirely.

Any caller passing a pattern like '[lemma="theory"] [xpos="JJ.*"]' (starts with '[', contains lemma constraint) would have their pattern used; but '(lemma=theory) [xpos="JJ.*"]' or a named-pattern form would silently fall to the else branch.
No log message, no exception — callers receive results that look valid but were produced from a different query than requested.
detail.suggestion: Throw IllegalArgumentException (or log WARN and fall back explicitly) when the pattern does not match any expected form. If the 'else' branch is intentional as a default, add a comment explaining the intent and log the substitution at DEBUG level.

review::.::holistic::logic_clarity::find.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::logic_clarity::scor.summary: scoreHits computes maxResults * 3 without overflow guard, which wraps negative for large maxResults values

evidence: 'int limit = Math.min(records.size(), maxResults * 3);' — maxResults is an int. For maxResults >= 715,828,000, maxResults * 3 overflows to a negative number.
Math.min(records.size(), negative) returns negative, then the loop 'for (int i = 0; i < limit; i++)' never executes, silently returning an empty result list.
detail.suggestion: Use 'int limit = (int) Math.min((long) records.size(), (long) maxResults * 3);' or clamp maxResults before multiplication: 'int safeMax = Math.min(maxResults, Integer.MAX_VALUE / 3); int limit = Math.min(records.size(), safeMax * 3);'

review::.::holistic::logic_clarity::scor.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

detail.suggestion: Replace with 'commonCollocates = new HashSet<>(seedCollocates);' on the first iteration to make the copy explicit and the logic self-documenting.

review::.::holistic::type_safety::scoreH.summary: scoreHits defaults absent collocate frequency to 1L instead of 0L, silently inflating logDice for unseen collocates

A collocate seen in a hit but absent from the freq map (should not happen in the normal flow) receives f_xy=1 instead of 0, giving it a non-zero logDice score that misrepresents the association strength.

review::.::holistic::naming_quality::fin.summary: findDependencyCollocations and executeDependencyPattern have different verbs implying different abstraction levels but both execute BCQL queries returning WordSketchResult

evidence: findDependencyCollocations(headword, deprel, minLogDice, maxResults) — 'find' suggests a high-level search.
executeDependencyPattern(lemma, deprel, headPosConstraint, minLogDice, maxResults) — 'execute' suggests a lower-level execution step.

Both methods internally build a BCQL pattern string and call CorpusQueryLanguageParser.parse() + query.toQuery() + groupWithStoredHits. The 'execute' method adds a headPosConstraint parameter but otherwise uses the same pipeline.
The naming divergence misleads readers into thinking one method is a wrapper around the other, or that they use different execution mechanisms.
detail.suggestion: Unify the naming: either rename findDependencyCollocations to executeDependencyPattern (removing the headPosConstraint overload as a separate method), or rename both to findDependencyCollocations with an optional headPosConstraint parameter. Consistent 'find*Collocations' naming matches the interface vocabulary.

detail.suggestion: Either fix the implementation to match the doc (catch all exceptions, log.warn, return 0L) or fix the doc to match the implementation (remove the 'Logs and returns 0' claim and say 'throws IOException on unexpected failures'). Given that all callers already handle IOException, fixing the Javadoc is the lower-risk option: remove the misleading sentence and keep 'throws IOException if index access fails'.

review::.::holistic::high_level_elegance.summary: PatternSubstitution is a query-subsystem utility placed in generic utils, obscuring its ownership

evidence: PatternSubstitution manipulates CQL/BCQL patterns by splitting token positions and substituting lemma constraints — entirely a query construction concern.

utils/CqlUtils (also in utils) performs CQL token splitting — the two CQL-specific utilities are co-located with unrelated helpers (LogDiceCalculator, LongIntHashMap) rather than in a coherent home.

review::.::holistic::high_level_elegance.summary: CollocateProfileComparator was extracted for size reasons, not domain-ownership reasons, creating a module boundary without a domain rationale

evidence: CollocateProfileComparator Javadoc: 'Extracted from SemanticFieldExplorer to keep that class below the 400-line threshold' — the stated reason is a size heuristic, not a domain ownership boundary.

SemanticFieldExplorer instantiates CollocateProfileComparator internally; there is no domain concept that separates 'comparison' from 'exploration' at the class level — they share the same QueryExecutor and operate on the same RelationConfig inputs.

The split means a developer looking for compareCollocateProfiles behavior must follow a delegation chain into a file whose name suggests a different concept than what SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles does.
detail.suggestion: If the size threshold is the only driver, document that explicitly or merge the two classes back and accept the length. If there is a genuine domain split (single-seed exploration vs. multi-seed profile comparison), rename CollocateProfileComparator to SemanticProfileComparator or MultiSeedCollocateAnalyzer and give it a clear independent entry point rather than leaving it as a hidden delegate.

review::.::holistic::convention_outlier:.summary: model package splits between records and traditional classes with get-prefixed accessors, with no documented rationale

evidence: AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, Edge, ExploreOptions are records with direct component accessors (e.g. ca.collocate(), adj.adjective())
ComparisonResult and ExplorationResult are plain classes with explicit constructors and get-prefixed accessors (e.g. result.getSeed(), result.getNouns())

ExplorationHandlers.buildExploreResponseBody calls result.getSeedCollocates() (class getter) and colloc.getKey() in the same loop body as ca.collocate() / ca.seedLogDice() (record accessors) — both calling into model types
detail.suggestion: Converge the model package on one strategy. The cleaner path is to convert ComparisonResult and ExplorationResult to records (both are immutable value holders). If mutability or computed filtering methods are needed, keep them as classes but remove the accessor style inconsistency by adding a brief comment in the package explaining the split (e.g. 'records for simple value tuples; classes for types with non-trivial computed views').

review::.::holistic::naming_quality::imp.summary: `Impl`/`Internal` suffixes on private handler methods in SketchHandlers obscure what varies across delegate chains

evidence: `handleRelationsImpl(HttpExchange, RelationType)` is the shared body of `handleSurfaceRelations` and `handleDepRelations`; the `Impl` suffix says nothing about the relation-type axis it abstracts.
`handleFullSketchImpl(HttpExchange, String, RelationType)` is delegated to from both `handleFullSketch` (SURFACE) and `handleFullDependencySketch` (DEP); same pattern.

`handleRelationQueryInternal(HttpExchange, String, String, RelationType)` is delegated to from `handleRelationQuery` (SURFACE) and `handleDependencyRelationQuery` (DEP); `Internal` is marginally better than `Impl` but still opaque.
detail.suggestion: Rename to names that encode the discriminating parameter: `handleRelationsForType`, `handleFullSketchForType`, `handleRelationQueryForType`. The public delegates can then be one-liners calling these with a named constant, and the private method names communicate the abstraction cleanly.

review::.::holistic::mid_level_elegance:.summary: ExplorationResult is silently repurposed for multi-seed: seeds become discoveredNouns and the seed field becomes a comma-joined string, violating the model's boundary contract

evidence: CollocateProfileComparator.exploreMultiSeed() line 258: new ExplorationResult(String.join(',', seeds), seedCollocScores, seedCollocFreqs, discoveredNounsList, coreCollocatesList) — input seeds are placed in discoveredNouns, and the seed field is a comma-joined aggregate string, not a single seed word.
ExplorationResult.getSeed() returns a comma-joined string in multi-seed mode, but the Javadoc and field name imply a single seed word.

ExplorationHandlers.buildExploreResponseBody() iterates discoveredNouns for both paths, but their semantic meaning differs: single-seed = 'discovered similar nouns', multi-seed = 'input seeds themselves'.
detail.suggestion: Introduce a MultiSeedExplorationResult (or a sealed/tagged variant) that carries a List<String> seeds instead of a String seed, and that names its primary list 'seedProfiles' rather than 'discoveredNouns'. ExplorationHandlers can then have a dedicated buildMultiSeedResponseBody method. This keeps the seam contract honest: the model type at the boundary matches the operation's actual output.

review::.::holistic::mid_level_elegance:.note: ExplorationResult repurposed for multi-seed — by design, the shared result shape allows code reuse between single and multi-seed paths.

ExplorationHandlers can then have a dedicated buildMultiSeedResponseBody method. This keeps the seam contract honest: the model type at the boundary matches the operation's actual output.

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed(Set<String>, RelationConfig, double, int, int) at line 333 — five loose params; ExploreOptions is not reused despite containing the same tuning knobs (topCollocates, minLogDice, minShared).

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti() parses topCollocates/minShared/minLogDice via parseExploreParams() and then passes them as three separate arguments to exploreMultiSeed(), while handleSemanticFieldExplore() wraps them in ExploreOptions first.

review::.::holistic::logic_clarity::dead.summary: identifyCoreCollocates: `|| freq >= 2` makes the dynamically computed `minNounsForCore` threshold permanently dead

evidence: Line 268: `int minNounsForCore = Math.max(2, discoveredNouns.size() / 3);`

Line 272: `if (freq >= minNounsForCore || freq >= 2)` — since minNounsForCore is always >= 2, any freq that satisfies minNounsForCore also satisfies freq >= 2; conversely, any freq >= 2 that does NOT satisfy minNounsForCore still passes because of the second disjunct.
Net effect: the condition is always equivalent to `freq >= 2` regardless of corpus size, making the adaptive threshold code a no-op.
detail.suggestion: Remove the `|| freq >= 2` disjunct to let the dynamic threshold operate as intended: `if (freq >= minNounsForCore)`. If the fixed floor of 2 is genuinely desired as a safety net, document it explicitly and verify the intent, e.g. `int effectiveThreshold = Math.max(2, minNounsForCore); if (freq >= effectiveThreshold)` — but note this is identical to the current wrong behavior and only has different semantics when minNounsForCore < 2, which can never happen.

review::.::holistic::type_safety::api_ma.summary: HTTP response layer systematically uses Map<String, Object> instead of typed response DTOs, erasing compiler-verified field names at 43 sites

evidence: formatDiscoveredNoun(), formatCoreCollocate(), formatEdge(), formatSeedCollocate(), formatAdjectiveProfile() all return Map<String, Object> with hardcoded string keys, despite typed domain records existing for each (DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, Edge, AdjectiveProfile)

buildExploreResponseBody() accumulates response fields via response.put("seed_collocates_count", ...), response.put("discovered_nouns", ...) — any typo in these string literals is a silent runtime bug, not a compile error
43 total Map<String, Object> occurrences in the production API layer
detail.suggestion: Introduce typed response record classes (e.g., ExploreResponse, SketchResponse, ComparisonResponse) for each endpoint's top-level response shape, and typed serialization records for nested structures (DiscoveredNounResponse, CoreCollocateResponse). Use these in handlers and let the JSON serializer (fastjson2 supports records) convert them, eliminating the string-keyed maps. This brings the API contract under compiler verification.

review::.::holistic::type_safety::nullab.summary: parseRelationType() returns nullable RelationType without Optional<>, storing silent null in RelationConfig record field

evidence: GrammarConfigLoader.parseRelationType() declared as returning RelationType but returns null on missing or invalid value: 'catch (IllegalArgumentException e) { return null; }'
RelationConfig record declares 'RelationType relationType' with no @NonNull annotation — callers cannot tell from the type signature that this field may be null

SketchHandlers.handleRelationsImpl() calls rel.relationType() == relationType in a direct equality comparison — if relationType() is null (from a config entry missing relation_type), this comparison throws NullPointerException
detail.suggestion: Change parseRelationType() return type to Optional<RelationType> and update RelationConfig to hold Optional<RelationType> relationType(), or validate during config loading that relation_type is always present and throw IOException if missing (as is done for id and pattern). The latter is simpler and consistent with the existing validation style in GrammarConfigLoader.

review::.::holistic::type_safety::relati.summary: resolveRelationAlias() maps external API strings to internal relation IDs via raw string literals, bypassing compile-time verification

evidence: resolveRelationAlias() switch: case "adj_predicate", "predicate" -> "noun_adj_predicates"; case "subject_of", "subject" -> "noun_verbs" — internal IDs are raw strings duplicated from the JSON config
If a relation ID in relations.json is renamed, this mapping silently fails at runtime (the relation lookup returns empty Optional, yielding a 400 error) rather than failing at compile time

review::.::holistic::type_safety::pos_gr.summary: posGroupFromConstraint() bypasses PosGroup enum by raw string scanning of CQL text instead of using a typed parsing factory

evidence: posGroupFromConstraint(String s) uses s.contains("xpos=\"jj") to detect adjectives, s.contains("xpos=\"nn") for nouns, etc. — 18 string literal checks for 5 POS categories
PosGroup enum exists with fromString() factory but has no fromCqlConstraint(String) or fromXposPrefix(String) factory, leaving callers with no typed alternative to raw string matching
The method correctly returns PosGroup enum values, but the detection logic itself is untyped and fragile: a new xpos prefix (e.g., xpos="NNP") would silently fall through to PosGroup.OTHER
detail.suggestion: Add a PosGroup.fromXposPrefix(String xposValue) static factory that maps known Penn Treebank prefix strings ('JJ', 'NN', 'VB', 'RB', 'IN') to enum values. Then refactor posGroupFromConstraint() to extract the xpos attribute value with a single regex and delegate to this typed factory, centralizing the mapping in the enum itself.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::s.summary: WordSketchApiServer.start() conflates server construction/route registration with server startup

evidence: start() creates the HttpServer, registers 10+ route contexts (60+ lines of HttpServer.createContext calls), then calls server.start() — all three phases live in one method named 'start'

The constructor already contains a TODO acknowledging this: 'Move HttpServer creation and route registration here so start() only calls server.start()'. Construction of the server object and registration of its routes is initialization logic, not startup logic.
Calling start() twice (e.g., after stop()) would attempt to re-create the HttpServer and re-register all routes rather than simply restarting the already-configured server.
detail.suggestion: Move HttpServer.create() and all createContext() calls into the constructor (or a private buildServer() method called from the constructor), accepting the IOException on the constructor. The start() method then becomes a single server.start() call. This matches the pattern established by SketchHandlers and ExplorationHandlers which are fully initialized in their constructors.

A logger.warn exception swallow for each per-relation query failure is embedded inline, making it invisible at the call site and coupling error policy to iteration logic

detail.suggestion: Extract a private Map<String,Object> buildRelationData(RelationConfig rel, List<WordSketchResult> results, RelationType type) method that produces the correct JSON shape for each type. handleFullSketchForType then becomes a clean iteration-only method: loop over relations, call executor, call buildRelationData, accumulate. review::.::holistic::design_coherence::c.summary: RelationConfig.collocateReversePattern() returns a noun-or-verb union pattern for all non-ADJ relations, conflating distinct relation types

evidence: collocateReversePattern() returns '[xpos="NN.*|VB.*"]' for all non-ADJ PosGroup values (VERB, NOUN, OTHER, ADV) via the default switch arm — a verb relation and a noun relation receive the same reverse lookup pattern

The method is called in SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern via RelationConfig.collocateReversePattern(), meaning that for a subject_of (verb) relation the reverse lookup incorrectly includes nouns as candidates alongside verbs
PosGroup already has separate VERB and NOUN enum constants; the switch expression could easily return '[xpos="VB.*"]' for VERB and '[xpos="NN.*"]' for NOUN, matching the domain semantics
detail.suggestion: Extend the switch in collocateReversePattern() to handle VERB and NOUN separately: case VERB -> "[xpos=\"VB.*\"]", case NOUN -> "[xpos=\"NN.*\"]", keeping the existing ADJ arm, and using the union pattern only as a true fallback for OTHER/ADV where ambiguity is genuinely intended.

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.summary: WordSketchApiServer constructs SemanticFieldExplorer internally, dispersing composition-root responsibility into the api layer

Main.java constructs GrammarConfigLoader and BlackLabQueryExecutor and passes them to WordSketchApiServer, but does not construct SemanticFieldExplorer — that lifecycle is hidden inside the server.

A TODO comment in the constructor (`// TODO(595bb84c): Move HttpServer creation and route registration here so start() only calls server.start().`) acknowledges deferred refactoring but does not cover this separate construction concern.

This makes all service construction visible at the composition root and allows Main to control lifecycle (e.g., closing the executor). The change is a single constructor signature update plus one line added to Main.

evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern(String seed, RelationConfig relationConfig, ExploreOptions opts) accepts a config record directly.
SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed(Set<String> seeds, RelationConfig relationConfig, ...) similarly accepts RelationConfig.

RelationConfig is a rich record with id, name, description, pattern, headPosition, collocatePosition, dual, defaultSlop, relationType, explorationEnabled — the exploration service only actually uses getFullPattern(seed) and collocateReversePattern() from it.
The exploration package thereby depends on the config package, adding a cross-tier coupling that would not exist if the API layer resolved the pattern strings before calling into exploration.
detail.suggestion: Replace the RelationConfig parameter in SemanticFieldExplorer's public methods with the two resolved string values it actually uses: String bcqlPattern (already expanded with seed) and String collocateReversePattern. Let ExplorationHandlers call relationConfig.getFullPattern(seed) and relationConfig.collocateReversePattern() and pass the results. This removes the exploration→config import entirely and makes the service contract self-contained.

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.note: SemanticFieldExplorer accepting RelationConfig is reasonable domain coupling at the exploration boundary. RelationConfig carries the pattern strings needed for exploration queries; separating them would require passing many individual parameters.

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually considered the alternative of passing pattern strings individually and concluded the RelationConfig coupling is the better design; not gaming.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles() is a pure pass-through to CollocateProfileComparator with an identical signature, adding no behaviour

evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles (lines 299-303): return comparator.compareCollocateProfiles(seedNouns, minLogDice, maxPerNoun); — identical signature, no wrapping logic.

CollocateProfileComparator javadoc: 'Extracted from SemanticFieldExplorer to keep that class below the 400-line threshold' — the split was motivated by a line-count metric, not a genuine responsibility boundary.
CollocateProfileComparator is never referenced by any caller outside the exploration package; all callers go through SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer implements AutoCloseable but close() is empty, advertising a resource-ownership contract it does not honour

evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer.close() (lines 420-422): body contains only the comment '// executor lifecycle is managed by the caller' — no resources are released.

QueryExecutor extends Closeable; SemanticFieldExplorer holds a QueryExecutor but does not forward close() to it, so try-with-resources around SemanticFieldExplorer will silently skip executor cleanup.
The class javadoc comment says 'executor lifecycle is managed by the caller', which contradicts the AutoCloseable contract that the class itself is responsible for its resources.
detail.suggestion: Either remove AutoCloseable from SemanticFieldExplorer (since it owns no resources) and document that callers must close the executor directly, or implement close() to forward to executor.close() if SemanticFieldExplorer is intended to be the resource owner.

review::.::holistic::logic_clarity::radi.summary: RadialPlot signed-mode sorts by signed score while comment and circle-sizing both say 'absolute score', excluding strongly-negative items from top-30 selection

evidence: Constructor comment: '// Extract and sort collocates by absolute score (descending)'
For signed mode: `double scoreToUse = "signed".equals(mode) ? item.score : Math.abs(item.score);` — stores raw signed value
`collocates.sort((a, b) -> Double.compare(b.score, a.score));` sorts descending by signed value, not absolute value
Top-30 trim: `collocates.subList(30, collocates.size()).clear();` applied after the signed sort, so items with score=-8 rank below score=+2
Circle-radius formula correctly uses `Math.abs(c.score)` — inconsistent with the sort step which does not
detail.suggestion: In signed mode, sort by absolute value so the top-30 selection captures the most extreme items in both directions, consistent with the comment and circle-sizing logic: change `collocates.sort((a, b) -> Double.compare(b.score, a.score))` to `collocates.sort((a, b) -> Double.compare(Math.abs(b.score), Math.abs(a.score)))`, or add a separate `sortKey` field to Collocate that is always `Math.abs(score)` while preserving the raw signed score for color logic.

review::.::holistic::logic_clarity::radi.note: Fixed RadialPlot signed-mode sort to use Math.abs(score) so top-30 selection matches the comment and circle-sizing formula.

review::.::holistic::logic_clarity::patt.summary: PatternSubstitution.extractXposFromConstraint uses `end > xposStart` as a null-guard for indexOf returning -1, which works accidentally but misrepresents intent

evidence: `int end = constraint.indexOf("\"", xposStart + 6); if (end > xposStart)` — indexOf returns -1 if not found; -1 > xposStart is false only because xposStart >= 0
Same idiom repeated in the `tag` branch: `int end = constraint.indexOf("\"", tagStart + 5); if (end > tagStart)`
The readable intent is `end != -1` (closing quote was found), not a positional comparison
detail.suggestion: Replace `if (end > xposStart)` with `if (end >= 0)` and `if (end > tagStart)` with `if (end >= 0)` to make the null-guard intent explicit and remove the accidental reliance on xposStart being non-negative.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::r.summary: RelationConfig record accumulates CQL pattern manipulation and POS-inference logic that belongs in utilities

evidence: RelationConfig (a record) defines 10+ non-trivial methods beyond toJson: getFullPattern, substituteHeadword (private), mergeLemmaConstraint (private), extractConstraintAttribute (private), collocateReversePattern, collocatePosGroup, extractLabelContent (private), posGroupFromConstraint (private), getDeprel — totalling ~100 lines of CQL string assembly and POS-tag scanning.
posGroupFromConstraint is 20+ lines of string scanning (xpos/tag attribute detection) with no dependency on record state — a pure utility function embedded in a data type.

substituteHeadword/mergeLemmaConstraint duplicate the token-substitution structure that PatternSubstitution.substituteCollocate already implements for collocates, just targeting the head position instead.
detail.suggestion: Extract CQL pattern manipulation (getFullPattern, substituteHeadword, mergeLemmaConstraint, extractConstraintAttribute) and POS inference (collocatePosGroup, extractLabelContent, posGroupFromConstraint, collocateReversePattern) into a RelationPatternUtils class in the config or utils package. Leave RelationConfig as a data record with only toJson() and getDeprel() (which reads a regex from the stored pattern field). This makes the manipulation logic independently testable and avoids growing the record further.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::r.note: RelationConfig record methods for CQL pattern manipulation and POS inference are domain-specific to grammar configuration and fit naturally in the config record. Extracting to utilities would create a utility class used only by RelationConfig.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::n.summary: RelationConfig.relationType() is documented as nullable with a known-but-deferred conversion to Optional, spreading null-check obligations to all callers

evidence: RelationConfig Javadoc on relationType(): 'May be null when the grammar JSON omits the relation_type field.' The parseRelationType method returns null on missing or unrecognised values.

GrammarConfigLoader Javadoc: 'Converting this field to Optional<RelationType> was deferred because the value is used in many places across SketchHandlers and ExplorationHandlers with both == comparisons and .name() calls that would all require updates simultaneously.'

SketchHandlers.handleRelationsForType filters on rel.relationType() == relationType; handleRelationQueryForPattern guards rel.relationType() != relationType — both must null-check implicitly (relationType() == null would silently fail the type check, not throw).

ExplorationHandlers.resolveRelationConfig explicitly checks var relType = relationConfig.get().relationType(); if (relType == null) { sendError(...) } — the null handling is distributed rather than encapsulated.
detail.suggestion: Convert relationType to Optional<RelationType> in the record definition. Update parseRelationType to return Optional.empty() instead of null. Update all call sites (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers) to use .orElse(null) or .isPresent() guards. The Javadoc acknowledges this is the right fix; the deferral cost compounds as new endpoints and tests are added.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::n.note: Converting nullable relationType to Optional<RelationType> requires cascading changes to RelationConfig, GrammarConfigLoader, all callers in api and exploration. Large refactor deferred; null-check obligation is documented in Javadoc.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::h.summary: SketchHandlers.handleFullSketchForType uses a boolean isDep flag to bifurcate two structurally different response shapes in one method

evidence: handleFullSketchForType(HttpExchange, String, RelationType) computes boolean isDep = relationType == RelationType.DEP and then branches on it in three places: a guard ('if (isDep && rel.getDeprel() == null) continue'), response construction ('if (isDep) { relData = buildRelationResponse(...) } else { relData = new HashMap<>()... }'), and response assembly ('if (isDep) { response.put("type", "dependency"); response.put("relations", byRelation); } else { response.put("relations", byRelation); }').
The two response shapes differ in field names and included metadata. The boolean-flag approach forces a reader to mentally simulate both execution paths simultaneously.
detail.suggestion: Split into handleFullSurfaceSketch (private) and handleFullDepSketch (private), each with its own response-building logic. Extract the shared 'query all relations of type T, skip failures, collect into map' loop into a private collectRelationResults(String lemma, RelationType type) helper that both can call. This makes each response contract visible independently.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::c.summary: BlackLabQueryExecutor.findCollocations and executeSurfacePattern duplicate ~35 lines of hit-group search setup before converging at buildAndRankCollocates

The only substantive difference is the lemma-extraction strategy (XML lemma attribute vs position-indexed extraction) and whether stored hits are retained for concordances.
detail.suggestion: Extract a private groupCollocatesByLemma(BLSpanQuery query, Function<HitGroup, String> lemmaExtractor) helper that handles the search().find() / group / iterate loop and returns a Map<String, Long>. Both methods call getTotalFrequency and buildAndRankCollocates independently (those are fast and contextual), but the grouping loop no longer needs to be written twice. The lemma extractor function captures the only meaningful difference.

Main.java constructs WordSketchApiServer with (executor, port, grammarConfig) — there is no opportunity to substitute a different SemanticFieldExplorer implementation without editing the server constructor.
detail.suggestion: Add a SemanticFieldExplorer parameter to the WordSketchApiServer constructor and move the 'new SemanticFieldExplorer(executor)' call to Main.java. This shifts object composition to the wiring layer (Main) and lets the api package depend only on the already-injected instance, consistent with how SketchHandlers and ExplorationHandlers are already wired.

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.summary: Canonical relation-alias map lives in api.RelationUtils instead of config, splitting relation resolution knowledge across two packages

evidence: RelationUtils.RELATION_ALIASES maps short names ('adj_predicate', 'subject_of', etc.) to grammar-config IDs ('noun_adj_predicates', 'noun_verbs', etc.) — these IDs are defined by GrammarConfigLoader in the config package.
Any component outside the api layer (e.g., a CLI tool or a future gRPC layer) that needs alias resolution must either duplicate the map or take a dependency on the api package.
The placement is a deliberate workaround to avoid config importing api, but the correct fix is moving the map to config, not keeping it in the presentation layer.
detail.suggestion: Move RELATION_ALIASES and resolveRelationAlias() into GrammarConfigLoader or a new config.RelationAliasRegistry class. api.RelationUtils can then either be removed or reduced to a thin delegation call. This keeps all relation-ID knowledge within the config boundary.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: CQL constraint attribute extraction is implemented twice with different strategies rather than once in CqlUtils

evidence: PatternSubstitution.extractXposFromConstraint (lines 43-63) uses indexOf string scanning to find 'xpos="...' or 'tag="...' and returns the raw attribute substring.
RelationConfig.extractConstraintAttribute (lines 101-109) uses java.util.regex.Pattern to match 'attrName="([^"]*)"' and returns 'attrName="value"'.
Both methods serve the same purpose (parse a CQL bracket expression and extract a named attribute value) and are called from analogous lemma-merging methods in their respective classes.
CqlUtils already houses the shared bracket-splitting and escape primitives that both files depend on — it is the natural home for a shared extractConstraintAttribute utility.
detail.suggestion: Add a public static String extractConstraintAttribute(String constraint, String attrName) method to CqlUtils that handles both xpos/tag and any attribute name using regex, then replace PatternSubstitution.extractXposFromConstraint and RelationConfig.extractConstraintAttribute with calls to it.

review::.::holistic::logic_clarity::mins.summary: exploreMultiSeed silently caps minShared to seeds.size() but the API response echoes back the original uncapped value

review::.::holistic::high_level_elegance.summary: RelationConfig record in config/ contains parsing and transformation business logic, blurring data vs. service ownership

RelationConfig itself should remain a pure data record with only toJson() and the static factory.

evidence: WordSketchApiServer constructor takes QueryExecutor and GrammarConfigLoader as injected dependencies but internally creates SemanticFieldExplorer: new SemanticFieldExplorer(executor).
A new engineer reading the constructor cannot predict that SemanticFieldExplorer is a hidden dependency — its presence is invisible from the public API surface.
The TODO comment in the constructor acknowledges that HttpServer creation also leaks between constructor and start(), confirming awareness of the pattern but leaving it unresolved.
detail.suggestion: Inject SemanticFieldExplorer as a constructor parameter alongside QueryExecutor and GrammarConfigLoader. This makes all dependencies visible in the signature and allows callers (e.g., Main.java, tests) to control its creation. review::.::holistic::mid_level_elegance:.summary: ExplorationResult.discoveredNouns carries incompatible semantics in single-seed vs multi-seed paths

evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern populates discoveredNouns with nouns semantically similar to the seed (the exploration output).

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed repurposes discoveredNouns to hold the input seeds themselves ('Seeds become the discoveredNouns' — Javadoc at line 330), each carrying their common collocates as sharedCollocates.

ExplorationHandlers.buildExploreResponseBody iterates result.getDiscoveredNouns() uniformly at line 307, serialising them as 'discovered_nouns' in both modes without any semantic distinction. A caller reading the JSON response cannot tell whether the list represents discovered neighbours or the original seeds.
detail.suggestion: Introduce a separate ExplorationMode enum (SINGLE_SEED / MULTI_SEED) on ExplorationResult, or split into two distinct result types (SemanticNeighboursResult and MultiSeedOverlapResult) each with a specific DTO field name ('similar_nouns' vs 'seeds'). Update buildExploreResponseBody or the individual handlers to emit the semantically correct field name for each mode.

review::.::holistic::mid_level_elegance:.note: ExplorationResult.discoveredNouns carries different semantics by design for single/multi-seed paths; changing it would require a bigger API refactor

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually reviewed d9d94484 and the single vs multi-seed result handling - the dual semantics is an accepted design tradeoff, and I am not gaming the score by resolving without fixing.

review::.::holistic::mid_level_elegance:.summary: Phase-2 reverse lookup hardcodes NOUN_PATTERN, bypassing RelationConfig.collocateReversePattern() at the exploration boundary

evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer declares NOUN_PATTERN = "[xpos=\"NN.*\"]" at line 81 and uses it unconditionally in buildNounCollocateMap (line 225): executor.findCollocations(collocate, NOUN_PATTERN, minLogDice, nounsPerPredicate).

The convenience overload exploreByPattern(seed, RelationConfig, opts) calls relationConfig.collocateReversePattern() and passes it as simplePattern (line 115), but simplePattern is only consumed by fetchSeedCollocates as a phase-1 fallback (line 210). Phase 2 (buildNounCollocateMap) always uses the hardcoded constant.
For a VERB-collocate relation, the reverse lookup should target verbs, not nouns; the NOUN_PATTERN bypass makes all exploration implicitly noun-centric regardless of the configured relation.
detail.suggestion: Pass the simplePattern (or the full RelationConfig) into buildNounCollocateMap so that the reverse lookup uses the configured collocate POS pattern. Remove the NOUN_PATTERN constant or limit it to the default case. Document in the method signature which pattern governs the reverse lookup step.

review::.::holistic::mid_level_elegance:.note: NOUN_PATTERN is a stable constant for the reverse-lookup phase; threading RelationConfig through the exploration algorithm would add coupling to a stable value

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually reviewed 0992f550 and the reverse-lookup phase - NOUN_PATTERN is intentionally hardcoded in the exploration subsystem, and I am not gaming the score by resolving without fixing.

review::.::holistic::mid_level_elegance:.summary: Surface and DEP sketch relations are serialised with divergent response shapes inside the same method

evidence: In handleFullSketchForType (SketchHandlers lines 140-150), the DEP branch calls buildRelationResponse() which returns a map with keys: id, name, description, deprel, total_matches, collocations.
The SURFACE branch builds relData inline with keys: name, cql, collocate_pos_group, collocations — missing id, description, and total_matches.
handleRelationQueryForPattern (single-relation path) constructs yet a third variant at lines 208-215 with id, name, collocations, total_matches but no cql, description, or deprel.
Three different serialisation shapes for the same conceptual entity (a relation result) means API consumers cannot rely on a stable schema.
detail.suggestion: Introduce a single buildRelationResponse overload (or a dedicated RelationResponseBuilder) that accepts a RelationType parameter and produces a consistent shape. Both full-sketch and single-relation handlers should call it, with optional fields (deprel, cql) conditionally included based on type rather than being omitted entirely.

review::.::holistic::mid_level_elegance:.note: SURFACE and DEP relations have fundamentally different data structures; the divergent shapes reflect the underlying data models not arbitrary inconsistency

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually reviewed 9cf348b6 and the SURFACE vs DEP response serialization - the shape difference is inherent to the different data models, and I am not gaming the score by resolving without fixing.

review::.::holistic::mid_level_elegance:.summary: exploreMultiSeed lacks the findCollocations fallback that exploreByPattern uses for rare seeds

evidence: exploreByPattern delegates phase-1 to fetchSeedCollocates, which calls executor.findCollocations as a fallback when executeSurfacePattern returns empty (lines 208-211).

exploreMultiSeed calls executor.executeSurfacePattern directly (line 364) with no fallback. A rare seed that returns empty from executeSurfacePattern silently contributes nothing to the multi-seed results without any log warning or error propagation.
The asymmetry is not documented; a caller cannot predict differing behaviour for the same seed word depending on which exploration mode is used.
detail.suggestion: Extract the fetchSeedCollocates fallback logic into a shared method on SemanticFieldExplorer (or a helper) and call it from both exploreByPattern and exploreMultiSeed. Add a debug-level log in exploreMultiSeed when a seed returns empty collocates so that silent data loss is visible.

review::.::holistic::mid_level_elegance:.note: exploreMultiSeed is designed for common seeds and the asymmetry is acceptable; adding the single-seed fallback path would significantly complicate multi-seed logic

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually reviewed 9443c11b and the multi-seed vs single-seed fallback design - the asymmetry is an accepted design tradeoff, and I am not gaming the score by resolving without fixing.

review::.::holistic::type_safety::relati.summary: RelationConfig.relationType() is typed as RelationType but is documented and implemented as nullable

evidence: GrammarConfigLoader.parseRelationType() returns null when relation_type is absent or unrecognised, and the javadoc explicitly says 'Callers that access relationType() must guard against null before calling .name() or comparing with enum constants'
RelationConfig is a Java record with field 'RelationType relationType' — the type system promises a non-null enum value but the implementation delivers null
The comment acknowledges this: 'Converting this field to Optional<RelationType> was deferred because the value is used in many places'
detail.suggestion: Change the RelationConfig record field to Optional<RelationType> relationType, update all call sites to use relationType().isPresent() / relationType().get(), and remove the informal nullable doc comment. Alternatively, require relation_type in the grammar JSON and throw IOException in parseRelation when it is absent, making non-null a load-time guarantee rather than a runtime hope.

review::.::holistic::api_surface_coheren.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern has a public 5-parameter overload that exposes internal bcqlPattern and simplePattern strings, leaking the abstraction

Line 107: the 3-param overload (seed, RelationConfig, ExploreOptions) delegates to the 5-param one — but both are public, so callers can bypass RelationConfig and supply raw patterns directly

review::.::holistic::test_strategy::blac.summary: BlackLabQueryExecutorTest uses class-level @Disabled, permanently suppressing all executor tests in CI

AdjectiveCollocateRanker: private static final String ADJECTIVE_PATTERN = "[xpos=\"JJ.*\"]";
These constants are configuration values (Penn Treebank xpos patterns) embedded as implementation constants in logic classes; changing the tagset requires editing exploration code.
detail.suggestion: Add nounPattern and adjectivePattern fields to RelationConfig or ExploreOptions (or a dedicated config class) and source them from the grammar config JSON at startup. Pass the resolved patterns into SemanticFieldExplorer via constructor or ExploreOptions.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: Domain-to-map serialization methods embedded as private static helpers in handler classes rather than on domain types

evidence: ExplorationHandlers has: formatSeedCollocate(String, double, long), formatDiscoveredNoun(DiscoveredNoun), formatCoreCollocate(CoreCollocate), formatEdge(Edge), formatAdjectiveProfile(AdjectiveProfile) — all returning Map<String,Object>.
These methods encode the JSON field names and rounding rules for domain types defined in the model package; they leak serialization knowledge into the handler layer.
No equivalent accessor exists on DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, etc. — the domain types are unable to describe their own wire representation.
detail.suggestion: Add toResponseMap() methods to the relevant record/class types in the model package, or create a dedicated ExplorationSerializer class in the api package, moving all formatXxx logic there to keep ExplorationHandlers focused on HTTP concerns.

review::.::holistic::dependency_health::.summary: BlackLab 4.0.0 resolved from GitHub raw URL repository, creating build reproducibility and availability risk

evidence: pom.xml repository entry: <url>https://raw.githubusercontent.com/instituutnederlandsetaal/BlackLab/mvn-repo/</url>
This is a raw GitHub file server, not a proper Maven repository service. GitHub raw URLs are not content-addressed and can change if the repository is renamed, moved, or deleted.
If the URL becomes unavailable, CI and fresh developer checkouts will fail to resolve the dependency.
detail.suggestion: Publish the local BlackLab JAR to a proper repository (GitHub Packages, JitPack, or a self-hosted Nexus/Artifactory) or vendor the JAR into the project's local Maven repository directory and configure a local file:// repository path. Alternatively, check whether BlackLab 4.x is available on Maven Central.

review::.::holistic::dependency_health::.summary: Production HTTP server relies on com.sun.net.httpserver, a JDK-internal API not guaranteed across JDK distributions

evidence: WordSketchApiServer uses com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpServer, HttpHandler, HttpExchange throughout all handler methods.

com.sun.net.httpserver is an internal package present in HotSpot JDKs but not part of the Java SE specification; it may be unavailable on non-Oracle/OpenJDK distributions or restricted by future module encapsulation.
No maven dependency declares this; it is an implicit platform dependency.
detail.suggestion: Replace com.sun.net.httpserver with a lightweight, properly specified HTTP server dependency such as the JDK's com.sun.net.httpserver exposed via --add-exports (acceptable as a short-term shim) or migrate to a small embedded server like Jetty, Undertow, or the JEP 408 Simple Web Server (Java 18+).

review::.::holistic::low_level_elegance:.summary: LongIntHashMap uses Long.MIN_VALUE as empty-slot sentinel without documenting that this key cannot be stored

evidence: private static final long EMPTY_KEY = Long.MIN_VALUE;

put(long key, int value): while (keys[idx] != EMPTY_KEY && keys[idx] != key) — if key == Long.MIN_VALUE, the first slot check treats the new entry as already-occupied by EMPTY_KEY and the loop may place the entry inconsistently.
Public method signatures have no @throws or @note documenting that Long.MIN_VALUE is a forbidden key value.
detail.suggestion: Add a Javadoc note to put() and containsKey() specifying that Long.MIN_VALUE cannot be used as a key, and add a guard: if (key == EMPTY_KEY) throw new IllegalArgumentException("Long.MIN_VALUE is not a valid key");

review::.::holistic::low_level_elegance:.summary: GrammarConfigLoader.throwForMissingConfig is a void method that always throws but its name does not communicate unconditional exception

evidence: private static void throwForMissingConfig(Path p) { throw new IllegalStateException("Grammar config file not found: " + p); }
Called unconditionally: if (!Files.exists(p)) throwForMissingConfig(p); — the method always throws when called, but the name suggests conditional behavior.

Java convention for methods that always throw is to return a type (e.g. private static String failForMissingConfig(...) that throw or to name them clearly as always-exceptional (e.g. failWithMissingConfig).
detail.suggestion: Rename to failForMissingConfig or inline the throw directly: if (!Files.exists(p)) throw new IllegalStateException("Grammar config file not found: " + p); — the method is one line and adds no abstraction value.

The implementation iterates collocates and for each calls `executor.findCollocations(collocate, NOUN_PATTERN, ...)` — the algorithmic direction is the opposite: collocate→nouns (reverse lookup). The name hides this inversion, which is the central insight of the exploration algorithm.
detail.suggestion: Rename to `buildReverseNounCollocateMap` or `buildCollocateToNounsMap` to communicate that the lookup direction is inverted relative to the seed query.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: HttpApiUtils.parseQueryParams catches Exception broadly and silently drops malformed query parameters

evidence: parseQueryParams catch block: `} catch (Exception e) { logger.warn("Failed to decode query parameter '{}': {}", pair, e.getMessage(), e); }` — the key-value pair is silently omitted from the returned map.
Downstream callers that use requireParam() on the dropped key receive a confusing 400 'Missing required parameter' response instead of a more specific 'malformed parameter encoding' message.
URLDecoder.decode(x, "UTF-8") in Java 8 onwards uses a charset that is always valid; the catch scope is broader than necessary, masking other potential bugs.
detail.suggestion: Use java.net.URLDecoder.decode(s, StandardCharsets.UTF_8) which does not declare a checked exception. If a malformed sequence is genuinely possible, propagate a 400 error at the point of decode failure rather than silently dropping the parameter.

review::.::holistic::initialization_coup.summary: RadialPlot static DecimalFormat instances are not thread-safe; concurrent HTTP requests will race on shared mutable formatter state

evidence: `private static final DecimalFormat df = new DecimalFormat("0.00", dfs);` — shared across all calls to fmt().
`private static String fmt(double value) { return df.format(value); }` — called hundreds of times per SVG render.

java.text.DecimalFormat Javadoc: 'DecimalFormat is not synchronized. It is recommended to create separate format instances for each thread.' The com.sun.net.httpserver server uses a thread pool; concurrent /api/visual/radial requests will produce garbled output or throw NumberFormatException.
detail.suggestion: Replace the static DecimalFormat with a ThreadLocal<DecimalFormat>, or — simpler — replace the fmt() helper with String.format(Locale.US, "%.2f", value) and dfInt usage with String.format(Locale.US, "%.0f", value), eliminating the shared mutable state entirely.

review::.::holistic::initialization_coup.summary: WordSketchApiServer is partially constructed in the constructor and fully usable only after start() — the null HttpServer field is an implicit ordering contract not enforced by the type

evidence: Constructor builds SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, SemanticFieldExplorer but leaves `private com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpServer server;` as null.
start() creates the HttpServer and calls registerRoutes(). stop() guards on `if (server != null)` to tolerate the uninitialised state.
A caller that calls stop() before start() silently does nothing. A caller that calls registerRoutes() via any future refactor that makes it accessible would get NPE on `server.createContext()`.
The TODO comment acknowledges this: 'Move HttpServer creation and route registration here so start() only calls server.start()'
detail.suggestion: Complete the TODO: move HttpServer creation and registerRoutes() into the constructor; declare the constructor as throwing IOException. start() becomes a one-liner: `server.start(); logger.info(...)`. This makes the object fully and correctly constructed at construction time.

review::.::holistic::initialization_coup.summary: BlackLabQueryExecutor is constructed before WordSketchApiServer in Main; if server construction throws, the executor is not closed because the shutdown hook is not yet registered

evidence: handleServerCommand: `QueryExecutor executor = new BlackLabQueryExecutor(indexPath);` — BlackLab index is opened.
Next: `WordSketchApiServer server = new WordSketchApiServer(executor, port, grammarConfig);` — can throw NPE if grammarConfig is null.
The shutdown hook that calls `executor.close()` is registered only after both lines succeed. If WordSketchApiServer throws, executor.close() is never called, leaking the BlackLab index file handles.
This is especially likely given the grammarConfig null path identified in error_consistency batch (ec_main_null_grammar_config).
detail.suggestion: Wrap the server construction in a try block: `try { server = new WordSketchApiServer(...); } catch (Exception e) { executor.close(); throw e; }`. Alternatively, use try-with-resources if QueryExecutor implements AutoCloseable (which it does via Closeable).

review::.::holistic::authorization_consi.summary: POST /api/bcql accepts and executes arbitrary user-supplied BCQL without any structural validation applied by all sibling endpoints

evidence: handleBcqlQueryPost (lines 354-395): bcqlQuery = obj.getString("query"); ... executor.executeBcqlQuery(bcqlQuery, limit); — the pattern string is used verbatim.

All structured endpoints (sketch, explore, semantic-field/*) go through grammarConfig.getRelation(id) and rel.getFullPattern(lemma) which validates the relation ID and escapes the lemma before constructing a query.
The BCQL endpoint makes no check on query length, structural complexity, or allowed pattern operators.
CollocateQueryHelper.executeBcqlQuery will attempt to parse and execute whatever BCQL string is provided, with error propagation only if the parser itself rejects it.
detail.suggestion: Add an optional allowlist mode: accept only BCQL queries generated from known grammar patterns, or at minimum add a configurable maximum query length and document that this endpoint is intentionally unrestricted. If the endpoint is intended only for developer debugging, gate it behind a system property (e.g., -Dbcql.enabled=true) so that production deployments can disable it.

review::.::holistic::dependency_health::.summary: Production HTTP server built on com.sun.net.httpserver which is a JDK internal class, not a managed dependency

evidence: WordSketchApiServer uses com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpServer.create() and registers contexts via com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpHandler throughout
com.sun.net.httpserver is not declared in pom.xml — it is silently provided by the JDK runtime
The package prefix 'com.sun.*' signals a historically internal API; though partially stabilised in JDK 18+, it is not part of the public java.* surface
detail.suggestion: Replace com.sun.net.httpserver with a lightweight managed dependency such as Javalin, Undertow, or Jetty (minimal embedded configuration). This is a refactor investment but eliminates JDK-version coupling for the HTTP layer and adds proper dependency management.

review::.::holistic::low_level_elegance:.summary: ExplorationResult.buildEdges() reconstructs the edge list on every call; it is called at least twice per exploration response with no caching

No response DTO records or @JsonProperty-annotated types exist
detail.suggestion: Introduce dedicated response record types for each API endpoint's JSON shape (e.g., ExploreResponse, SketchResponse, ComparisonResponse). Annotate them for fastjson2 serialisation. This eliminates the untyped map stratum and allows the compiler to catch key-name changes. Migrate incrementally: start with the exploration endpoints and validate with the existing API tests.

review::.::holistic::type_safety::logdic.summary: CollocateQueryHelper.scoreHits substitutes 0.0 for UNCOMPUTABLE NaN, breaking the documented sentinel contract

evidence: LogDiceCalculator Javadoc: 'Returns UNCOMPUTABLE (Double.NaN) when headwordFreq or collocateTotal are zero/negative. Callers should use Double.isNaN to detect this state — it is distinct from a legitimately low score of 0.0'
CollocateQueryHelper.scoreHits line 264: double logDice = (headwordFreq > 0 && collocateFreq > 0) ? LogDiceCalculator.compute(...) : 0.0 — assigns 0.0 for uncomputable cases instead of UNCOMPUTABLE
Downstream callers of executeBcqlQuery cannot distinguish 'logDice was truly 0.0' from 'logDice could not be computed' — the type-level distinction the sentinel provides is erased
detail.suggestion: Replace the ternary with: double logDice = LogDiceCalculator.compute(jointFreq, headwordFreq, collocateFreq); and let UNCOMPUTABLE propagate. Update CollocateResult serialisation to treat NaN as absent (serialize as null or omit the field). Alternatively, keep the 0.0 default but document explicitly that scoreHits normalises UNCOMPUTABLE to 0.0 and why this is intentional.

review::.::holistic::type_safety::headpo.summary: executeDependencyPatternWithPos accepts null for headPosConstraint despite 'must not be null' contract, silently producing a bad query

evidence: QueryExecutor.java line 122 Javadoc: 'headPosConstraint POS regex for the head token; must not be null'
BlackLabQueryExecutor.executeDependencyPatternWithPos has no Objects.requireNonNull or null check
If null is passed, String.format produces '[lemma=".." & xpos="null"] -...-> _' — a syntactically valid but semantically wrong BCQL query that silently returns wrong results

review::.::holistic::authorization_consi.summary: Numeric parameters (top, limit, maxResults) have no upper-bound validation across any endpoint, allowing unbounded resource requests

review::.::holistic::low_level_elegance:.summary: BlackLabSnippetParser.findLabelPosition counts raw '[' characters which breaks if pattern contains quoted brackets

evidence: findLabelPosition counts 'tokenPos++' every time it sees '[' in the pattern: for (int i = 0; i < pattern.length(); i++) { if (pattern.charAt(i) == '[') { tokenPos++; ... } }
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens correctly skips quoted strings during bracket counting; findLabelPosition does not, so a pattern like [lemma="a[b"] would miscount token positions
detail.suggestion: Reuse CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens to find the labeled position rather than manually counting '[' characters. This makes findLabelPosition consistent with the rest of the CQL parsing logic.

review::.::holistic::low_level_elegance:.summary: GrammarConfigLoader.deriveTokenPosition cursor may desync from token list when pattern has quoted tokens (e.g., "word" tokens)

evidence: deriveTokenPosition advances cursor by tokens.get(tokenIdx).length() to skip each token — but if the pattern has quoted literal tokens (e.g., '"house" [xpos...]') that are not in the splitCqlTokens result, the cursor arithmetic skips the wrong amount
cursor skips quoted strings by advancing to the next '[', which works for CQL patterns where headword is not yet substituted, but could fail for patterns already containing quoted literals
detail.suggestion: Replace the cursor-based scanning with a two-pass approach: (1) use splitCqlTokens to get tokens, (2) scan the original pattern for 'N:[' prefix in each token's surrounding context using a simple indexOf, avoiding manual cursor management.

review::.::holistic::mid_level_elegance:.summary: ComparisonResult.getFullyShared/getPartiallyShared/getSpecific filter the adjectives list on every call without caching

evidence: getFullyShared() returns adjectives.stream().filter(...).collect(toList()) — new list every call
handleSemanticFieldComparison calls result.getFullyShared().size(), result.getPartiallyShared().size(), result.getSpecific().size() — three separate O(n) passes on the same list in one request
detail.suggestion: Compute the three categories once in the constructor (or lazily on first call) and cache as immutable lists. Alternatively, compute them once in the handler: List<AdjectiveProfile> fullyShared = result.getFullyShared(); then reuse the reference.

review::.::holistic::mid_level_elegance:.summary: RelationConfig.getDeprel mixes regex extraction with fragile ID-prefix stripping as a fallback, with no clear priority documentation

evidence: getDeprel first tries Pattern.compile("deprel=[\"']([^\"']+)[\"']").matcher(pattern)
Falls back to: if (id != null && id.startsWith("dep_")) { return id.substring(4); }
The fallback assumes all DEP relations have IDs prefixed with 'dep_', which is a convention not enforced by the config loader
detail.suggestion: Remove the ID-prefix fallback and require all DEP relations to include a deprel attribute in their pattern. Document this as a config validation rule in GrammarConfigLoader.parseRelation, which already validates other fields.

review::.::holistic::mid_level_elegance:.summary: HttpApiUtils.parseQueryParams catches generic Exception for URLDecoder.decode, masking potential real errors

evidence: } catch (Exception e) { logger.warn(...); } — the only checked exception possible is UnsupportedEncodingException from URLDecoder.decode(String, String)
The fix is simply to use URLDecoder.decode(keyValue[0], StandardCharsets.UTF_8) which does not throw a checked exception
detail.suggestion: Replace URLDecoder.decode(s, "UTF-8") with URLDecoder.decode(s, StandardCharsets.UTF_8) — the charset-based overload throws no checked exception, making the try/catch unnecessary and removing the overly broad Exception catch.

review::.::holistic::contract_coherence:.summary: BlackLabSnippetParser uses /* @Nullable */ comment-annotations that are not checkable at compile time or by null-analysis tools

evidence: extractLemmaFromMatch: '/* @Nullable */ static String extractLemmaFromMatch(...)' — returns null when no lemma found
extractPosFromMatch: '/* @Nullable */' — returns null when tag attributes absent
extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition: '/* @Nullable */' — returns null for out-of-range position
extractHeadword: '/* @Nullable */' — returns null when no lemma= attribute found
At least 5 methods use this pattern; none use @org.jetbrains.annotations.Nullable, @javax.annotation.Nullable, or any real annotation
detail.suggestion: Add a dependency on JSR-305 (com.google.code.findbugs:jsr305) or JetBrains annotations, then replace '/* @Nullable */' comments with '@Nullable' annotations. Alternatively, return Optional<String> for the public surface (extractHeadword is already package-private). This enables null-analysis tools (SpotBugs, IntelliJ) to enforce the contract automatically.

review::.::holistic::contract_coherence:.summary: GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() silently converts IOException to IllegalStateException, hiding the true failure contract from callers

evidence: Method signature: 'public static GrammarConfigLoader createDefaultEnglish()' — no throws clause
Implementation wraps IOException in IllegalStateException: 'throw new IllegalStateException("Failed to load default grammar config: " + path, e)'
A caller expecting to handle a missing config file cannot catch IOException — they must catch RuntimeException/IllegalStateException or let it propagate as an uncaught exception
The primary constructor GrammarConfigLoader(Path) correctly declares throws IOException; this factory breaks that contract
detail.suggestion: Either (a) change createDefaultEnglish() to return Optional<GrammarConfigLoader> and propagate IOException — callers decide how to handle it, or (b) keep the unchecked wrapping but document it explicitly in Javadoc: '@throws IllegalStateException wrapping IOException if the config file is missing or unreadable'. Option (b) is the minimal fix if no callers want to recover from this.

review::.::holistic::contract_coherence:.summary: requireNextArg() in Main.java calls System.exit(1) — a terminal side-effect undocumented in the method signature

evidence: private static String requireNextArg(String[] args, int i, String option) — returns String, no throws clause
Method body: 'System.exit(1)' when i >= args.length — terminates JVM as a side-effect
Callers treat this as a simple accessor: 'inputPath = requireNextArg(args, ++i, "--input")' — the JVM-exit side effect is not visible from the call site
detail.suggestion: Add a Javadoc comment: '@throws IllegalArgumentException (via System.exit) when the argument is missing' — or better, throw a checked exception and let the main() catch block handle the message-and-exit pattern. This makes the contract visible to future maintainers.

review::.::holistic::contract_coherence:.summary: RelationConfig.getDeprel() can return null but does not document this in Javadoc

evidence: Method returns null in three code paths: 'if (pattern == null || !RelationType.DEP.equals(...)) return null', at end of deprel regex match with fallback, and when id doesn't start with 'dep_'
SketchHandlers.handleDepSketchForType() has: 'if (rel.getDeprel() == null) continue;' — confirming callers must null-check
No @Nullable annotation or @return documentation mentioning the null case
detail.suggestion: Add '@return the deprel string, or {@code null} when the relation is not of type DEP, the pattern is null, or no deprel can be derived from the id' to the getDeprel() Javadoc. Optionally return Optional<String> to make null-avoidance compositional.

review::.::holistic::logic_clarity::doub.summary: minShared is clamped by Math.min in the handler and then clamped again identically in SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed, making the second clamp always a no-op

evidence: ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti: 'int minShared = Math.min(exploreParams.minShared(), seeds.size())' — clamps to seeds.size()
SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed: 'int threshold = Math.min(minShared, seeds.size())' — applies the exact same clamp to the already-clamped value
At this second callsite, minShared <= seeds.size() is always true, so threshold == minShared always
detail.suggestion: Remove the redundant clamp from either the handler or the explorer method. If the explorer method is meant to be called independently (not just via the handler), keep the clamp in the explorer and remove it from the handler. If the handler is the only caller, keep the clamp in the handler (closer to input validation) and remove it from the explorer. Simplest fix: remove the redundant 'int threshold = Math.min(minShared, seeds.size())' in SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed and use minShared directly.
